title: Celtis brevifolia
taxonomy: Celtis brevifolia is classified in the family Cannabaceae, within the order Rosales. It belongs to the genus Celtis, which includes other species of hackberries. The genus Celtis is a group of deciduous and evergreen trees and shrubs native to the Northern Hemisphere. The Cannabaceae family, although commonly known for Cannabis, also includes several other plant genera. This classification places Celtis brevifolia within the broader context of flowering plants.
uses: Celtis brevifolia, also known as the short-leaf hackberry, has been traditionally used in folk medicine for its various health benefits. Its bark, leaves, and roots are believed to possess antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties, which may help in treating various ailments. The plant is said to have been used to treat fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions. However, more scientific studies are needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of its medicinal uses. In traditional medicine, the plant is often used in combination with other herbs to enhance its therapeutic effects.

It has been traditionally used to treat various ailments, such as fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues. constituents: Celtis brevifolia, also known as the desert hackberry, contains a variety of active constituents that contribute to its medicinal properties. These constituents include flavonoids, alkaloids, and phenolic acids, which have been found to possess antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Flavonoids, such as quercetin and kaempferol, are known to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects, while alkaloids, such as berberine, have been shown to possess antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties. The exact composition of active constituents in Celtis brevifolia may vary depending on the source and preparation of the plant.

intro: Celtis brevifolia, also known as the dwarf hackberry or dwarf nettle tree, is a deciduous shrub or small tree native to the southwestern United States and northern Mexico. Traditionally, Celtis brevifolia has been used to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and skin irritations, due to its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of this plant include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and triterpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal effects. In herbal medicine, Celtis brevifolia is often prepared as a tea, tincture, or salve, with the most common preparations being an infusion for fever and skin conditions, and a topical application for wound healing and skin irritations.
